speaker: interviewer
text: Diament.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dana Schwartz.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a pleasure to work with you. You're a wonderful-- wonderful storyteller. I can see it. OK, Freddy. So you were in the brick factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And let me say this. The Blockfuhrer, the SS guards, or the-- the-- the-- the corporals and sergeants, that went with us, to supervise us at the factory, did change. And there were some real bastards. I mean, real, I would say, barbaric and-- and-- and murderous among them. And one of them was called [NON-ENGLISH] and one of them was called [NON-ENGLISH]. Later on, [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH], the steel. And they, instead of letting us work when we arrived there, did pure chicanery. I'll give an example. In the winter, when there was high snow, they gave a few of the-- or, half of the inmates shovels. And we had those pajama coats. And we had to open it up to put snow into this. And said, ran to a distance, let's say a hundred yards, and unload it. And later on, they loaded-- they-- they put it there, and ran to the other side. And they brought some regular guards. Not-- not sergeants or corporals, not SS fuhrers. And they were beating us up, literally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and-- I remember almost every day-- we had a platform, a wagon, that was drawn not by horses, but by human beings. You know? With-- with-- with-- we had some rope. And we had to-- to carry this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, we schlepped home a-- a-- a-- a-- a wagon full of corpses, or 90% dead already. They were literally beaten to death. It was going on, especially in the winter, when one of those SS fuhrers was assigned. And we-- in the morning, when we marched out, our first look was, who is in charge today. Who is in charge today. And we saw those mass murderers, those barbaric and-- and-- and-- and brutal, sadistic people. We know we bring the wagon full tonight. We-- we schlepped the wagon with us-- there was nothing on it-- just for the purpose of bringing home the ones that were half-dead, 3/4 dead, or dead. That was the order of the day. Also, we had-- at the Klinkerwerk, there was a little harbor. It was at the Spree. The Spree is a famous river in Berlin. Maybe you heard the name, the Spree. They unloaded cement-- because the bricks had also a certain amount of cement in it-- to unload cement sacks. And the cement sacks in Germany were 50 kilo. Not 50 pounds. That's very heavy. And if-- and then-- and then from the boat to the-- to the dry, there were planks that-- that-- that wide. And they took people that were totally emaciated to schlep those cement sacks. And they fell in the water. They drowned. And then-- and then-- and then before they drowned, they were shooting at them, also. They said, oh, you are escaping. Nobody made any attempt to flee, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, as I said, these deceptions. They call them, oh, wow, you want to-- you want to run away, huh? And shot him. Those people had to, of course, be schlepped home on those-- Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on-- on-- on those platforms. I want to say something else about-- oh yeah. That-- I work there, maybe for a year. Then, I-- I found a Jewish friend who was a painter. He was painting for SS the-- the-- the guard towers, you know? Those-- those towers with the machine guns. He was painting, also, some of the installations on the Klinkerwerk for the Germans, for the German factory. And he was from Hamburg. We became friends. And he said he's going to teach me to become a wallpaper-- a wall painter. And I worked for a few months as a painter, went on to painting. Yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your dad do? What did your father and brother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- my-- my brother was also in the Klinkerwerk. My father was assigned to chicanery kommandos. And they were beaten up. They were schlepping garbage to the garbage dump, and unloading, collecting garbage, all kinds of things. And I always tried very hard to get him assignment where the danger of being beaten was less. Sometimes I succeeded. And sometimes he was in a kommando, because I-- I-- I arranged with the kapo, and they took him out. I had no control over this. I saw him only at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw him, Freddy, what were the meetings like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were sleeping. By the way, in Sachsenhausen, we-- there was not enough equipment. We didn't sleep in bunks. We slept on the floor, like sardines. Head to foot, like sardines. 250, 300, in one giant room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep next to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother, yeah. That I could arrange. Because-- because the-- the-- the-- the guy that was in charge-- each barrack had two wings, A and B. And-- and then at the top was the Blockalteste. And the other one was Stubenalteste, they called it. Stube A-- Stube is like room-- and Stube B. And I had some protection there, and he sorted that I could sleep with my brother and my-- and my father. Also because I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to talk, whisper, to each other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. We talked. At night we talked. Oh yeah. Yeah. My-- my father was also praying every night. He was a religious Jew. And-- and he want us to pray. And we didn't pray anymore. We-- we lost all belief in-- in-- in-- in-- if a god can-- can allow this to happen, either there is no god or the god is not an all-- all-powerful and all-- more. But he was very, very religious and Orthodox, and he did this. He also was telling us, God is testing the Jews. You know, he developed theories. Like Job in-- in-- in the ancient Bible story. He's testing us. But eventually, like he always has saved the Jews over the centuries, he would save us, too. He believed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years. Let me tell you another very, very frightening experience. In '42-- no, in '41 already, late '41-- they suddenly brought thousands of Russian prisoners of war. And they built a special camp for them. Very primitive. They treated them worse than the Jews. They treat them so badly, they're literally-- if we were living, let's see, in a barrack, 500, they put thousand of them. There was no room not to stand, not to sit, not to lie down. And the food they gave them was very minimal, less than we got. And they had always a lot of SS guards watching them and shooting them. They-- they went berserk sometimes. It was terrible. They treated them worse than wild animals. I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do I know this? I was assigned for a period of three weeks-- I couldn't get out of this. Every morning, they'd put out the-- it was winter. It was cold-- their dead. Naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were like-- like-- like-- like boards. Like boards. All skeletons. And cannibalism. It was so bad, there was eaten up a hand-- a hand, there was eaten up a foot. It was terrible. And we had to load them on those wagons, that were drawn by my fellow inmates. On wagons. Like-- we stacked them like-- like wood, like planks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, why a hand or a foot where there is no meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever meat there was there, they ate it. There is little meat. It-- we heard the screaming at night, when they were-- were-- were-- were-- were rioting, or were-- they didn't-- I would assume they went berserk, they went crazy, the way they treated them. And they-- they-- they-- they rebelled. And they were shooting them. Like animals. Beating them and shooting them. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: These were prisoners of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners of war. Russian prisoners of war. Yeah. I would say, the time that I was in Sachsenhausen, over the period of a year and a half, at least 100,000 of them. And they all-- they all died. They all were killed and beaten to death. They never worked. They were beaten to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's what they did to them. And then later on, in-- in-- in-- in-- in late '42, they made experiments with gas on moving-- on vans, with the-- with the monoxide, you know? They did it not with Jews, with Russian prisoners of war. Did-- were you involved with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about it. I knew about it because I saw-- I work there. And I saw when the-- when-- when the vans came and loaded the prisoners. I saw it with my own eyes. And then, I had connections with political prisoners and they told-- they told us this. We also had some political prisoners-- Jews-- that were in the camps since '33. They were communists, socialist, and they tried to brainwash us to become communists. They did propaganda sorts of thing. Especially with the young people. They wanted to-- to-- to-- to-- tell us how good socialism is, how good communism is, and eventually the whole world will be saved only by communism, by socialism. And they-- they told us their ideology. They, they so to speak, lecture us. And they told us they had connections with the other communists. By the way, each camp had a hierarchy, internal, of Lageralteste, Stubenalteste, Blockalteste, kapos. And most of the officials in the camp were political prisoners in Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of those Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few. Yeah. But they-- the Jews had not higher positions. Only Germans had that. But-- but at least they had not lost their human touch. They tried to save us. They helped us to survive. And if you needed somebody, that-- an important person that want to save, you could somehow, through proteksia, arrange, that he was sent to a working kommando where the chances of chicanery and of murder was the least. Least. I mean, every day was a fight for survival. And-- and I'll give an example. If he came home from work, then sometimes those bullies, I call them, the SS bullies, came and said, oh, you ate, now let's do some sport. Within the barracks. And it was terrible. And I was-- I was so small-- the barracks were-- were built on a-- how do you call it-- about, I would say, this high. I-- I was small enough to crawl under the barracks. They couldn't see me. That-- that way I saved myself not being beaten. It was absolutely murderous. They didn't gas the people. Through chicanery, beating, and lack of medical treatment and lack of food. I give an example. It was so bad with the abscesses-- they were full of abscess-- that the SS agreed because the stink was so-- that the people couldn't stand it. There was, in-- in between the two wings, there was a latrine. They put the people in the latrine. They were lying on the floor and dying. Literally dying. And at night, people had to go to relieve themself, they're stepping on them. I mean, it-- it was unbelievable. In other words, there was no human-- they're totally dehumanized us. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember walking over people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- was unbelievable. They totally-- they did everything to take away any sense of humanity from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by then, you were not-- had no belief in God. And how-- how did you survive psychologically?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll give an example. Somehow, these-- those theories, those political theories, that they tried to-- to-- to lecture us. I said, maybe, when you are drowning, and there is a piece of straw, you catch it to. I start believing that eventually socialism will take over the world, the Nazis will-- they also said, the Nazis are not going to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eventually, the whole world-- those-- those-- those communists told us this-- the world will organize and there will be even the capitalist world, the communist worlds will get together, in order to survive, in order to defeat the Nazis. It might take years. The question is, who will be strong enough and lucky enough to survive this nightmare? You know-- So you had some hope for the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. That-- that was my hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and also, I had a will to survive. My brother, too. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to hear something? He believed that God will save us. That was his belief. He believed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were there from October 1939 until?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, there is-- when-- when [NON-ENGLISH] was killed, and when-- when-- a Nazi officer was killed, there-- there were-- so to speak, you had to pay for it. They took every fifth man, just like this, come out. And they shot him. Oh, they did this very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the worst part was that if somebody escaped, especially on the outside, kommandos, on the working kommandos, the whole camp has to stand, and no food, no-- no-- no-- no toilet, sometimes for 24 hours. On the parade ground, on the roll call yard. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- there, I-- I-- I told the story-- there were example were called spiritual resistance. We had, among our people, some prominent film directors. We had some professors from Holland, from Belgium, deported from Norway, all they brought to Sachsenhausen until '42. From the occupied territories in the West. From France. And there-- and I will never forget, there was one-- his name was Steiner. Either he was a film writer or a film director. He was telling us stories about how film might be made, to keep us alive, to-- to-- to-- to-- to not to give up. Because the minute that you collapse, or you-- you said, I have to sit down to lie down, the SS shot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was during the day or in the evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, as you were standing, after somebody escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And-- and we had a professor there from-- from-- from-- from-- from Holland, a philosopher-- philosophy professor-- gave us lectures about Spinoza, about Kant, about Descartes. Yeah. And-- and people listened to this. And it makes us easier to-- to-- to-- to have a will to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people could hear him? He didn't speak with a microphone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That-- we-- we were set close. Whoever was around him. I would say, a block was an average of 300. And we were all in-- in-- in rows of five, very tight together. And when we had to stand there, we were even-- in order to warm us up, we were holding each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, enough people heard him. Let's put it this way. Out of the 300, maybe a hundred listened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you have to stand there and not to do anything, you despair. So in order to keep us literally alive and resistant, and not to lose the will to survive, that's what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this became sort of a home to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I mean, I-- that-- that's where I grew up. My youth, so to speak. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got a little education, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I am a-- I am a top graduate of Sachsenhausen, so to speak. Yeah. The graduate school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, one day, they gave the order-- there were four Jewish barracks. And each barracks were, I would say, 350 to 400. And they said, we all have to go to the disinfection plant. There was-- you know, they-- they constantly were fighting lice, because the-- the sanitary conditions were-- were impossible. They-- they-- they give us underwear once every three months, you know, so you know. It was terrible. And-- and it was unavoidable that people got sick and there were-- there-- there were-- there was a typhus epidemic, where-- where-- where hundreds died from lice. And there was a disinfection plant where the clothes were disinfected-- our clothes-- and when people came, they took away their civilian clothes. They were also disinfected. And they were stored in the magazine, or they were sent to German, to Germany, for-- for Germans to be used. They sent us all to disinfection. So we got very suspicious that they might-- and-- and, as I said, then there were rumors already that gas chambers are being built. Not in Sachsenhausen, but there are other concentration camps where they have gas chambers. The rumors were spreading. Where they came from-- as I said, the political people in the inside had access to-- to-- to-- to radio. And even SS men sometimes, in their stupidity, told them what was going on. They-- they gained their confidence by manipulating those people. The prisoners that were there-- were-- there were in Sachsenhausen 60 members-- former members-- of the German parliament. They were all highly educated people. They were socialists and communists. They were professors, they were doctors, they were intellectuals. They somehow man-- worked also in the offices of the SS. They used slave labor to do office function, that normally women do, like writing letters, filing, cleaning, what have you. And through their contact with those people, they got information that normally was not supposed to be available. And-- and we heard rumors. And we were afraid they might really put us there in the disinfection chambers and kill us there. We were afraid of that. And we were there for two days and two nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In those disinfection area.

speaker: interviewer
text: They left you inside for two--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were big hangars. Not-- not-- not into-- into the chambers where the disinfectant area is. And-- and we-- we got scared. And the-- I told you there were some old-time Jewish communists, Jewish socialists, that went through hell with the Nazis in concentration camps since 1934, and survived. And they knew, also, people. They had connections. Interesting enough, the political prisoners, even if-- they were moved around from one camp to the other-- and they had connections. And-- and-- and they said, this looks here very ominous. And we are afraid that eventually-- they took us here for some purpose. They never told us for what purpose. Only Jews. That they had some feeling, that they eventually would kill us off. They would gas us. And they said, look. If we have to be killed, let's be killed like human beings. Not like animals. And we revolted. Believe it or not. We-- they took all the young people. The old people didn't want to go. And we all ran and screamed one afternoon, against the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the gate. And the SS was there. And for some reason, they didn't shoot us. Didn't shoot us. They just caught us. And then the Lager Fuhrer-- at that time it was not anymore [NON-ENGLISH], but somebody else-- told us that we will be deported to the east, to a labor camp, they said, not to a concentration camp. And you can't understand. It is, to me, it was very incomprehensible why they didn't kill us all. Because revolt is-- is punished by death. They apparently were compelled to send so and so many people to Auschwitz. And-- and-- and-- and he didn't probably want to admit to Berlin, to the Gestapo, that they were so ineffective that people could revolt. It's unheard of. It would have put a black-- it would have put his name in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in jeopardy. And SS officers that did not behave, and didn't do a good job, were sent to the front-- to the Eastern Front-- which meant certain death. And they didn't want to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is my explanation. That's the reason why they didn't kill us all. Because they have to give a report, there was revolt. I would like to ask you one more thing about the revolt. Do you remember how it started? Were you any part of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I told you. There were-- I even remember some of their names. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], they went around-- only the younger people-- among the younger people. We were actually at that time, at the disinfection--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hangar.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hangar. I would say, young people, at least about a hundred. From the age of 15 to 25. Maybe even 120. And he convinced us-- we were so demoralized to be there two days-- and the rumors, I mean, rumors were-- were growing day by-- hour by hour. And they said, we have the feeling that the actually intention of the SS [INAUDIBLE] is to kill us all. So if we get killed, why not get killed like heroes-- as in-- like human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to hear something? I-- I really was scared to death. I believed they really would kill us. They would gas us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and I said, if-- what difference does it make? Let's get killed by-- by-- by-- by shooting, at least, like man. Like human beings. And I-- my brother, too. Maybe-- we all ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father didn't run. The elderly people stayed there. They-- God will help them, so to speak. They-- they really believed in God, that-- that eventually will save them. And that-- and-- and they did not kill us. They calmed us down. Even they gave us food. I remember this like yesterday. To calm us down, that-- to show, so to speak, their good will, and-- and told us, next day, you will be all sent by train to a labor camp in Poland. That's the order that came from the headquarters, the Gestapo. You aren't the only ones. All the Jews in all the concentration camps are being deported to the east. So you didn't have to say goodbye to your father--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no, no. All of. All.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because you thought he was coming with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't say Auschwitz. But we knew that-- not Auschwitz. Auschwitz was really a Polish military castle, you know that. But Birkenau was built only '41. And how was Birkenau built? The former [NON-ENGLISH] of [NON-ENGLISH] became the commandant of Auschwitz, took with him 50 [NON-ENGLISH]. Before [NON-ENGLISH] means [NON-ENGLISH], professional killer. They had a green angle.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all-- look what the Nazis did-- when they built the concentration camps, they took all the prisoners in the-- in the prisons-- that had-- they were convicted for capital offenses-- rapists, murderers-- and made them kapos and Blockalteste in the camp. They became the big shots in the camp. They were themselves prisoners, enjoyed a lot of privileges, and stole half the Russians. They were dividing the Russians in the-- in the barracks. They stole everything and even sold it for-- for-- for cigarettes, for-- for-- for-- for alcohol, for liquor, what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took 50 of the toughest kapos and Blockalteste and took them to Birkenau. And they developed Auschwitz and Birkenau. And Rudolf Hess became, then, the commandant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but we knew, through the connection of the political prisoners, that they built an Auschwitz. They built a camp for the purpose of mass murdering Jews from all the occupied territories. That we knew. No details. Rumors. Nothing official. But-- and we believed those rumors. We believed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the-- the truth is, the next day, they took us all to-- to-- to-- to-- to-- to-- to the railway station, put us on-- on-- on, not-- on-- on regular cars, old cars. Very cramped, you know. When there was only seat for 50, put in a hundred.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took us, I think, 2 and 1/2 days. We arrived, one night, at Auschwitz. And then we knew already was Auschwitz. And then we knew already, Auschwitz is the camp where people are being gassed. Gassing was already going-- at that time, going on very intensively.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That we knew. Interesting enough, they had told us rumors, again, our-- our-- our leaders, so to speak, the-- the-- the-- the the-- the Communists function, so to speak, told us apparently-- we arrived on a Sunday-- apparently, the gas chambers are closed on Sunday. And they will take us to the camp. And we will have to work in the camps. But the first thing they did in Auschwitz is tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Show me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here. It's already a little bit distorted, after so many--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What's the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 69927. I always say it's my telephone number. [GERMAN]. You know, when-- when you were-- were-- were asked by an SS officer, you had to say, [GERMAN]. You had no name anymore. You were only numbers. We-- you had immediately to report to him who you are. [GERMAN]. That was the rule. You are just a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it hurt you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It-- it-- it's like, like when you get a-- a-- a-- an injection. It's really done by injection. It-- it's a needle and they're points, you saw this, didn't you? And that was done by Polish-- by Polish prisoners. At that time, there were lots of Polish prisoners-- political prisoners-- in Auschwitz. Later on, they were all deported. The-- only Jews stayed in Auschwitz. And they told us, please, don't pretend it hurts. You haven't-- you don't even know what hurts. You will find out what hurt. That is-- that is peanuts, so to speak, he told us. Oh, yeah. He prepared us all right. They were very cynical. And-- and-- and-- interesting enough, the Polish prisoners, although some of them were communists and socialists, they hated the Nazis and hated the Jews at the same time. They're anti-Semitic. Not all of them, but I feel the majority. As a matter of fact, I will tell you when we came to-- to-- to Auschwitz, the Polish kapos, how they behaved. But we're going to get to it. So anyway, that was our arrival in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- did you think that the next day, which was going to be Monday, you were going to be gassed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. We-- they told us right there, if you get a number, you are destined to work-- to work yourself to death, so to speak. And then they threw us in the shower-- cold shower. It was October. It was icy cold. And then, really, to dehumanize us, naked, we had to-- to dry out, outside the shower, in-- in-- in-- on a-- on a-- on a parade ground. I would say we-- we stood there at least for six hours, freezing to death. And then they-- then, for some reason, they organized-- everybody got a blanket. And they sent us to the barracks where we were assigned to. And I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop now, and we'll hear about Auschwitz in the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.
